exemplar.title: Investigating Dual Processing Theory Through Font Fluency in CRT Questions

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating Dual Processing Theory Through Font Fluency in CRT Questions

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim is clearly stated with explicit relevance to dual-process theory and real-life decision making.
Dual-process theory accurately described and linked to processing fluency in the investigation.
Independent and dependent variables are operationalized and hypotheses are well framed.
weaknesses: Minor typographical and attribution errors in the background (e.g., âKahmenâsâ typo, theory date).
Operationalization could be more precise (e.g., specific font types, scoring rubric for responses).
Hypothesis wording has slight grammatical clarity issues (e.g., âmean marksâ phrasing).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction excels in stating and explaining the aim, theory connection, and operationalization of variables, matching band-5 descriptors. Minor inaccuracies and room for more precise operational detail prevent a top score of 6, so 5/6 reflects its strong overall performance.

strengths: Research design is not only described but justified in terms of avoiding order effects and maintaining control.
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are identified with reference to task suitability.
Controlled variables and materials are listed and their relevance to data reliability is noted.
weaknesses: Justification for choosing independent measures focuses on ethics rather than practice/fatigue effects.
Discussion of convenience sampling lacks depth on external validity and potential biases.
Procedural anomalies (unexplained symbols) and ethical documentation explanations need clarification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section meets the âexplainedâ level across design, sampling, and materials but with limited depth and some misfocused justifications. This aligns with mid-band performance in a 0â4 scale, meriting 3/4.

strengths: Descriptive (means, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics are appropriately applied.
Graphical presentation is accurate, well-labelled, and directly addresses the hypothesis.
Findings are linked to p-values and the research hypothesis, showing clear interpretation.
weaknesses: Assumptions of the MannâWhitney U test are not discussed and sample sizes (n) are omitted from tables.
Interpretation equates mean scores with percentages without clear explanation of the conversion.
Graph could include error bars and justification for the non-parametric test choice.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis demonstrates high proficiency with correct statistics and flawless graphing. Minor omissions in reporting assumptions, sample sizes, and error bars keep it at 5/6 rather than full marks.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to dual-process theory and prior literature.
Strengths and limitations are relevant to the investigation and are described with reference to validity issues.
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and are logically justified.
weaknesses: Discussion lacks exploration of alternative explanations or deeper theoretical nuance.
Several validity claims are slightly misframed (e.g., ecological vs. internal validity).
Modification proposals could include more operational detail (e.g., pilot testing plans, stratification criteria).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation shows solid connection of findings to theory and well-justified modifications, corresponding to the upper end of band 3â4. Surface-level discussion depth and some misinterpretations prevent awarding higher than 4/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar chart is clearly labelled, depicts the correct descriptive values and directly represents the hypothesis (comparison of means). No noticeable errors are present; hence full marks in the top band are merited.

The statistical findings are stated, linked to the p-value, and explicitly used to reject the null and support the research hypothesis, with reference to descriptive data. Interpretation is accurate but somewhat brief, so 5 marks in the top band are justified.

Both IV and DV are correctly named and fully operationalised (easy vs difficult fonts; mean score out of five). A null and directional research hypothesis are provided. This matches the top-band descriptor with no evident shortcomings, warranting the top of the band.

Convenience sampling is described and partially justified (IB maths proficiency, ease of access). While the explanation of representativeness is brief, it does link the choice of participants to the nature of the task, satisfying the âexplainedâ descriptor, but with limited depthâso 3 marks within band 3-4.

Dual-process theory is accurately described (characteristics of each system, origins with Kahneman & Tversky) and the link to the present investigation is explicitly explained through the role of processing fluency. The explanation is generally clear, though not deeply critical, so band 5-6 is appropriate; 5 marks reflect the solid but not exhaustive treatment.

Findings are related to dual-process theory and the Alter & Oppenheimer study, but the discussion remains surface-level and does not explore alternative explanations or theoretical nuance. This aligns with the âdescribed with referenceâ descriptor (band 3-4). Awarding 3 reflects limited depth.

The student clearly states the aim (to investigate the effect of font readability on cognition) and explicitly explains why this matters in relation to dual-process thinking and real-life decision making. The relevance is not merely identified but explicitly expanded upon with reference to System 1/System 2 and previous findings. This satisfies the descriptor for band 5-6; minor elaboration gaps justify awarding 5 rather than 6.

Multiple strengths and limitations are identified and each is accompanied by a brief explanation linking to validity or generalisability, fulfilling the âstated and describedâ requirement. The reasoning is relevant though not highly sophisticated, so 4 marks (upper end of band 3-4) are appropriate.

Controlled variables (time, questions, instructions) are identified and their importance is briefly explained; materials are listed with clear relevance. The strand therefore fits band 3-4. Some explanations are perfunctory, justifying 3 rather than 4.

Each proposed modification is explicitly linked to a previously stated limitation and justified (matched-pairs for maths ability, stratified sampling, realistic maths problems). This meets the top-band descriptor; slight brevity prevents awarding the absolute maximum, so 5 marks are given.

The student not only describes but also explains why a laboratory, independent-measures design was chosen and why repeated measures was avoided. The justification demonstrates understanding, fulfilling the âexplainedâ criterion. Minor depth issues keep the score to 4 rather than the maximum of 4.

Means, standard deviations and a Mann-Whitney U test are applied correctly and match the design/scale of data. Calculations presented appear coherent; however, no check of assumption (ordinal level) is discussed, so a cautious 5 within the top band is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The aim is clearly stated but could be more precise by describing the specific font types and cognitive tasks used to operationalize readability.
Independent variable is named, but the fonts (âeasyâ vs âdifficultâ) should be defined (e.g., sans-serif vs cursive, point size) to improve operationalization.
Dependent variable is specified as mean marks, but clarify how each correct response is scored (e.g., 1 point per item) and how missing answers are treated.

The background section contains a typo in âKahmenâsâ and misdates the theoryâcorrect to âKahneman & Tversky (1974)â. Ensure accurate attribution and year of publication for dual-process theory.
The description of System 1 lists its features but does not link processing fluency to the experimental manipulation. Explicitly connect how font readability engages System 1 heuristics.
The section on System 2 correctly outlines analytic processing but could be improved by referencing how difficult fonts trigger reflective thinking in the present study.
When summarizing Alter & Oppenheimer (2007), the percentages are reversedâeasy-font participants had lower correct rates. Verify data and clarify sample characteristics.
Null hypothesis wording (âno significant difference toâ) is unclear. Rephrase as âno difference in mean scores between font conditions.â
The research hypothesis uses âhigher number of means marksâ; correct to âhigher mean marksâ. Ensure clarity and grammatical accuracy.
Justification for using independent measures focuses on ethics rather than order effects. Revise to discuss how this design avoids practice or fatigue effects.
Convenience sampling is described but its impact on external validity and potential biases should be discussed.
Alternating rows reduces discussion risk, but participants may still compare answers across conditions. Consider partitioning or physical barriers.
Materials are listed comprehensively; enhance by explaining how consent and debrief forms support ethical and reliable data collection.
The procedural step showing â\omegaâ and âÎ â¼ 10â´â seems out of place. Remove or clarify these mathematical symbols.
Double-scoring by four examiners is a strength that improves reliability. Consider reporting inter-rater reliability coefficients.
Descriptive table is clear; include group sample sizes (n) to complement means (M) and standard deviations (SD).
Interpretation equates means with percentage correct; clarify that M=1.5/5 corresponds to 30% correct and cite formula used.
The bar chart effectively compares means but could include error bars and label sample sizes for transparency.
Use of MannâWhitney U test is appropriate but discuss assumptions (e.g., ordinal data suitability) and why no parametric test was chosen.
The discussion links findings to dual-process theory but could explore alternative explanations (e.g., novelty of difficult font) to deepen evaluation.
Identifying order-effect elimination is insightful; could strengthen by noting random assignment checks or counterbalancing in future.
Convenience sampling is listed as strength, but it is better framed as a limitation due to limited generalizabilityârevise accordingly.
Describing lab context as high ecological validity is incorrect; clarify that a controlled setting trades ecological validity for internal validity.
The matched-pairs modification is well linked to variability limitation; explain how pairs would be matched (e.g., pretest scores) and sample size implications.
Stratified sampling suggestion is sound; detail the strata (e.g., proficiency bands) and discuss practical challenges in a single-school context.
Modifying questions to realistic math scenarios is valuable; strengthen by proposing pilot testing to calibrate difficulty levels.
Reference list inconsistent: apply a single APA style, include publication years, full titles, and retrieval dates for online sources.
